content.text: is the process of moving from local development to production infrastructure, ensuring security, scalability, reliability, and observability for AI agents to use your tools.
: Treat local servers like remote ones by containerizing them, especially for scaling:

# Production Dockerfile
FROM python:3.11-slim

# Install dependencies
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy server code
COPY . .

# Expose port for HTTP/SSE
EXPOSE 8080

# Run server
CMD ["python", "weather-server.py"]
: Isolated execution, no host dependency conflicts
Pin all dependencies to specific versions

# requirements.txt
mcp==1.2.0
anthropic==0.25.0
fastapi==0.109.0
# Never use: mcp>=1.0.0 (unstable)
: Unpinned versions cause silent breakages when dependencies update
instead of environment variables or config files:
: Store auth tokens in configuration files

# Bad: Token in config file
# config.json: {"api_key": "abc123"} ❌

# Good: Load from secrets manager
import boto3

client = boto3.client('secretsmanager')
response = client.get_secret_value(SecretId='mcp/weather/api-key')
api_key = response['SecretString']

# Production settings
API_VERSION = os.getenv("API_VERSION", "v1")  # Safe
MODEL_VERSION = os.getenv("MODEL_VERSION", "claude-3")  # Safe

# NEVER for secrets:
# API_KEY = os.getenv("API_KEY")  # ❌ Don't do this
: API versions, model names, ports, feature flags

# deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: weather-mcp-server
spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: weather-server
        image: my-registry/weather-mcp:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        env:
        - name: API_VERSION
          value: "v1"
        # Secrets from Secret Manager
        - name: API_KEY
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: weather-secrets
              key: api-key

---
# autoscaling.yaml
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: weather-server-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: weather-mcp-server
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70
: When CPU > 70%, Kubernetes adds pods (up to 10)
: ECS for containerized servers + Lambda for serverless:

# AWS CDK stack (simplified)
import cx = aws_cdk.Stack

class MCPServerStack(cx.Stack):
    def __init__(self):
        # ECS Fargate for containerized server
        ecs_service = ecs.FargateService(
            cluster=self.cluster,
            task_definition=task_def,
            port_mapping=8080
        )
        
        # Lambda for stateless endpoints
        lambda_fn = awslambda.Function(
            handler="mcp_handler",
            runtime=awslambda.Runtime.PYTHON_3_11
        )
: ECS handles long-running servers, Lambda handles bursts

name: MCP Server CI/CD

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  build-and-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Build Docker image
        run: docker build -t weather-mcp:${{ github.sha }} .
      
      - name: Run tests
        run: docker run weather-mcp:${{ github.sha }} npm test
      
      - name: Push to registry
        run: docker push my-registry/weather-mcp:${{ github.sha }}
      
      - name: Deploy to Kubernetes
        run: |
          kubectl set image deployment/weather-mcp weather-server=my-registry/weather-mcp:${{ github.sha }}
          kubectl rollout status deployment/weather-mcp
Deploy new version alongside old, switch traffic
Deploy to small subset, then all users
: Use canary or blue-green to validate updates with subset of users before full rollout
using short-lived, scoped tokens (not static API keys):

# AWS Cognito for OAuth 2.0
cognito_user_pool = cognito.UserPool(
    pool_id="weather-mcp-pool"
)

cognito_app_client = cognito.UserPoolClient(
    user_pool=cognito_user_pool,
    generate_secret=True,  # ← Client secret
    supported_login_auth=[
        cognito.LoginAuthenticationFlag.PASSWORD
    ]
)
with OAuth 2.0 Protected Resource Metadata endpoints:
: Standards-compliant with OAuth RFC 9728 for interoperability

# Stateless HTTP handler
@app.post("/mcp")
async def handle_mcp_request(request: dict):
    # No session state—each request is independent
    result = await process_mcp_message(request)
    return result

:
- ✅ Concurrent client support (no sticky sessions)
- ✅ Easy scaling (any server handles any request)
- ✅ Fault tolerance (no single-instance state)
via Let's Encrypt or cloud provider's load balancer:

# AWS CloudFront + WAF (SSL termination)
cloudfront_distribution = aws_cloudfront.Distribution(
    default_certificate=acm.Certificate.from_certificate_arn(
        "arn:aws:acm:region:account:certificate/abc123"
    ),
    web_acl=aws_wafv2.WebAcl(...)  # ← WAF protection
)
: Expose MCP servers without TLS (man-in-the-middle attacks)
at the gateway to prevent rug pulls, tool poisoning, and prompt injection:

:
| Check | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| Server signature verification | Ensure authenticity |
| Tool description scan | No malicious code |
| Rate limits | Prevent abuse |
) for orchestration to restart unhealthy instances:
to detect and restart failed pods

:
- High latency (> 2s)
- Failed requests (> 1%)
- CPU/memory limits exceeded
between agents and servers for observability, structured logging, and audit trails:

Agent → MCP Gateway → MCP Server
        ↓
    Structured Logs + Audit Trail

:
- ✅ Centralized logging
- ✅ Audit trails (who called what)
- ✅ Rate limiting
- ✅ Pre-flight checks
Ensure audit logs contain contextual metadata

# Good audit log
audit_log = {
    "timestamp": "2024-01-15T10:23:45Z",
    "user_id": "user_123",
    "client_id": "claude-desktop",
    "tool_name": "get_weather",
    "arguments": {"location": "SF"},
    "result": "success",
    "latency_ms": 150
}

# Bad audit log (just debugging)
audit_log = {"error": "Failed to fetch weather"}  # ❌ Missing context
: User ID, client ID, tool name, arguments, result, latency
regarding approvals, server inventory, and kill-switches to prevent shadow MCP activities:
: Users will adopt MCP with or without official consent—govern it proactively
: Smaller, well-defined toolsets result in faster, more cost-effective, and more reliable agents:
: Excessive permissions create risk zones for AI tools, especially with broad file/network access

# AWS CDK deployment (from AWS solutions)
# 1. Install CDK
npm install -g aws-cdk

# 2. Configure AWS
aws configure

# 3. Bootstrap CDK
cdk bootstrap

# 4. Clone source
git clone https://github.com/aws-solutions-library-samples/guidance-for-deploying-model-context-protocol-servers-on-aws.git
cd source/cdk/ecs-and-lambda

# 5. Install dependencies
npm install

# 6. Login to ECR
aws ecr-public get-login-password --region us-east-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin public.ecr.aws

# 7. Deploy entire stack
cdk deploy --all

# 8. Update MCP server
cdk deploy MCP-Server

:
- VPC (network)
- CloudFront + WAF (SSL + protection)
- ECS Fargate + Lambda (servers)
- Cognito (OAuth 2.0)
- OAuth metadata endpoints
: HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager—not env vars
: Kubernetes, ECS, Cloud Run, or Lambda
: User ID, client ID, tool, latency
: Keep humans in loop for sensitive actions
